text: Hello friends. This is a case of total laparoscopic hysterectomy.

The surgeon uses a ligature to manipulate a small bubble above the sacral promontory, ensuring it remains in position. The right round ligament is identified, located four centimeters lateral to the uterus, with the fallopian tube three centimeters and the ovarian ligament two centimeters from the uterus.
The surgeon cauterizes the ovarian ligament, which is visible near the fallopian tube, while addressing the anterior leaf of the broad ligament.

The assistant pushes the uterine manipulator towards the contralateral shoulder, maintaining a neutral position. The surgeon then addresses the left side upper pedicles, identifying the round ligament approximately four centimeters lateral to the uterus, followed by the fallopian tube three centimeters lateral, and the ovarian ligament. The surgeon uses a grasper to manipulate these structures, with ports positioned ipsilaterally and contralaterally for access.

The surgeon retroverts the uterus using a uterine manipulator, positioning it at a five, six, and seven o'clock orientation. The anterior leaf of the broad ligament is stretched with the left hand while the right hand cuts it, ensuring the ureter is moved away for safety. The bladder is then separated.

The surgeon separates the bladder from the uterus, revealing a crisscross pattern of vessels between the bladder and vagina. The bladder pillar is also separated on both sides, allowing the ureter to move laterally. The uterus is retroverted to facilitate the separation process.

So total laparoscopic hysterectomy is a very simple and easy surgery but the trick is that your task analysis should be perfect and all the steps of the surgery should be nicely defined. So this is the again retroverted uterus and it is done. After that another trick that you should remember one, twelve and eleven. So uterus should be completely antiverted and it should be at one o'clock position.

The surgeon stretches the posterior peritoneum with one hand and uses a harmonic or monopolar device to cut it, ensuring the incision is made above the arc of the uterosacral ligament. The uterine hump on the left side is visible, with the uterus positioned at one o'clock.

The uterus is positioned at eleven o'clock for cutting the right side of the posterior peritoneum. The surgeon stretches the peritoneum before cutting to avoid touching the vein with the harmonic tip, preventing bleeding. The posterior peritoneum is separated, aiding in moving the ureter away. The uterine hump on the right side is clearly visible.

The surgeon approaches the uterine pedicle, ensuring the bladder is separated and the anterior and posterior leaves of the broad ligament are opened. Using a ligature, the ascending part of the uterine pedicle is coagulated before moving to the junction, avoiding direct coagulation of the uterine artery. The pedicle is coagulated multiple times without cutting.

The surgeon coagulates the uterine artery on the right side, ensuring the tissue becomes paler as the blood supply is stopped. The procedure involves lateral and medial coagulation without cutting, followed by a second attempt where cutting occurs. The uterine artery is visibly coagulated, and irrigation is applied to prevent overcooking of dry tissue, with the eschar of the ligature being cleaned.

The surgeon cuts the left uterine pedicle and separates the cervicovaginal fascia using blunt dissection. The left side is completed, with visible coagulation of posterior veins to prevent attachment. The tip of the uterine is visible, and the uterus is pushed towards the contralateral shoulder to proceed with the right side. Saline and betadine are applied to the ligature jaw before coagulating and cutting the uterine pedicle posteriorly.

The surgeon secures the uterine artery, causing the uterus to become visibly pale, indicating cessation of blood supply. The colpotomy is performed using a harmonic device, with the assistant pushing the colpotomizer firmly to facilitate cutting. The vibrating tip of the harmonic aids in the precise incision, completing the colpotomy.

The anterior and left side of the colpotomy are complete, and the surgeon proceeds to the posterior part, carefully separating the uterosacral ligament without cutting the vaginal portion. The uterus is now free and is pulled through the vagina using the colpotomizer.

The surgeon performs a salpingectomy, applying anteromedial traction to the fallopian tube to separate it from the ovary. The tube is then pushed into the vagina. The procedure is repeated for the other tube, using a ligature to catch the infundibular ovarian ligament while maintaining proximity to the tube to avoid collateral damage to the ovary and artery.

The surgeon begins suturing the vaginal vault, taking sequential bites through the uterus, uterosacral ligament, and anterior and posterior vaginal walls. The assistant lifts the suture tail while the surgeon uses the left hand to support the suture and the right hand to take bites. The suturing includes the uterosacral ligament, forming a double-layer closure with a second row of sutures near the tail end.

The surgeon ties the final knot, ensuring the anterior and posterior vaginal fascia and epithelium are included to prevent granulation tissue formation. The vault closure is completed using various knot techniques, including extracorporeal square knot and continuous suturing.

The surgeon performs intracorporeal suturing to close the vaginal vault, starting with a surgeon's knot from the right side and continuing along the uterosacral ligament to the left side. The angles are approximated as the suturing returns to the starting point. Care is taken to hold the suture near the needle to avoid overshooting and potential injury to the iliac vessel or bowel.
The surgeon pulls a curved needle through a 5mm port, accompanied by the cannula to ensure the needle exits properly. Final suction irrigation is performed, completing the procedure.
